0: <title>Life Cycle Assessment of a Modern Northern Great Plains U.S. Swine Production Facility </title>
James J. Stone, Christopher R. Dollarhide, 1 Ramith Jinka, Robert C. Thaler, Chris E. Hostetler, and David E. Clay3 3

1Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, South Dakota School of Mines and Technology, Rapid City, South Dakota. Departments of 2Animal and Range Science and 3Plant Sciences, South Dakota State University, Brookings, South Dakota.
Received: February 15, 2010 Accepted in revised form: October 3, 2010
1: <header>Abstract </header>

A life cycle assessment (LCA) model was developed to determine the environmental impacts associated with a modeled modern Northern Great Plains U.S. swine production facility. This region has experienced significant growth (2.5 million head increase since 1996) and currently accounts for 13% of U.S. swine herd. The LCA model followed a "gate-to-gate" perspective focusing solely on feed manufacturing and swine production facility operations and did not include feedstuff production or abattoir and other postproduction supply chain pro- cesses. Model input data were normalized using a head of swine functional unit and were segregated into two operation processes and associated subprocesses: feed manufacturing including electricity use and completed feed transport, and production facility operations including manure handling and storage, enteric emissions, electricity generation and propane production, and water extraction. Four distinct operation scenarios were modeled, including normal operations, use of feed-based antimicrobials, a 50% reduced completed feed trans- portation distance, and a 30% increased use of renewable energy for all operations. For the normal operations scenario, climate change impacts were predominantly attributed to swine enteric emissions (331.6k CO2 eq) and manure storage and handling (53.4kg CO2 eq), whereas antimicrobial use reduced total climate change impacts by 3.7%. Manure storage and handling resulted in the greatest contribution toward marine eutrophi- cation (0.048 kg N eq) and terrestrial acidification (1.29 kg SO2 eq) impacts, whereas production facility electricity and heating resulted in the greatest terrestrial ecotoxicity (0.00060 1,4-DB eq) and freshwater eutrophication (0.00016kg P eq) impacts observed. Results from this LCA model provide Northern Great Plains swine pro- ducers and environmental practitioners with techniques that can be used to improve swine production effi- ciencies and identify opportunities for a future carbon-constrained and sustainability-aware marketplace.
Key words: swine; life cycle assessment; swine production; facility operations; climate change

3: <header>Introduction </header>

S INCE 1980, the number of swine production facilities in the United States has steadily declined from 660,000 to 69,000 facilities (Meyer, 2007). Although the total annual swine production units have remained relatively constant, a typical modern production facility has dramatically increased its animal unit holding size and annual throughput. Since 1996, a greater percentage of the annual pig crop has come from farms with >5,000 head (USDA, 2009a). By 2008, 50,700 hog operations had fewer than 99 head of swine and ac- counted for <1% of the U.S. swine inventory, whereas 2,900 operations had >5,000 head and accounted for >61% of the

U.S. swine inventory (USDA, 2009a). Although per capita pork consumption in the United States has not significantly changed since 1960 (22.2kg of boneless equivalent in 1960 compared with 21.1 kg in 2008), there has been a significant growth of the pork export market-by 2008 the United States exported 2,118 metric tons of pork (NPB, 2010). Increased pork production has also resulted in increased demand for products and processes associated with pork production such as feedstuff, water, energy, and manure handling operations. The gradual change toward larger swine production operations has brought much attention to the economic and environmental impacts of pork production. As the size of typical swine production facilities continue to grow, the magnitude of local greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions such as manure handling process may also increase; however, these larger facilities tend to operate more effi- ciently, reducing their regional environmental impacts on a per animal basis. Further, the emergence of a consumer

*Corresponding author: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, South Dakota School of Mines and Technology, Rapid City, SD 57701. Phone: +1 394 2443 Fax: +1 605 394 5171; E-mail: james.stone@sdsmt.edu

marketplace demanding sustainable management practices is now requiring the pork industry to better scrutinize their environmental burdens. The carbon footprint associated with U.S. pork production was identified as an emerging concern by the National Pork Board (Stokes, 2009), and this industry is now contentiously implementing methods to minimize GHG emissions and other associated environmental burdens.

There have been several recent studies investigating the carbon footprint and other life cycle-related impacts associ- ated with animal production processes. Most studies have focused on production within the European Union (EU) (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Eriksson et al., 2005; Nunez and Fermoso, 2005; Basset-Mens et al., 2007; Dalgaard et al., 2007; Edwards-Jones et al., 2009) or Australia (Peters et al., 2010; Wiedemann et al., 2010), and only few North American-based studies are currently available. Direct com- parisons between these studies and those presented within this or other North American-based studies can be challeng- ing due to (1) differences in genetic make-up of EU swine herd; (2) utilization of nontraditional (from a U.S. perspective) feedstuff; (3) typically less-efficient ventilation systems for EU facilities; (4) differences in market weight pigs as EU market pigs are generally lighter weight resulting in greater feed ef- ficiency gains; and (5) differences in manure management practices (Brewer et al., 1998). However, there have been a number of important findings from these studies, which have significant relevance for Northern Great Plains (NGP) com- prising of Minnesota, North Dakota, and South Dakota swine production operations. Feed choice, including related enteric emissions (Peters et al., 2010), and feedstuff production (Eriksson et al., 2005) were found to have major contributions toward life cycle environmental impacts. In particular, Eriksson et al. (2005) found that the use of soybean products (commonly referred to as soymeal in the United States) had significantly higher environmental production cost compared with other sources of crude protein such as peas and rapeseed meal. Elimination of soymeal resulted in reducing energy use by 10%, GHG emissions by 7%, and eutrophication impacts by 17%. Dalgaard et al. (2007) reported that feed production and manure handling were the highest contributions to global warming, eutrophication, and acidification-related impacts for Danish pig farming, in which, like NGP facilities, manure slurry is commonly applied to minimize or compliment syn- thetic fertilizer use.

The largest share of U.S. production resides in the Midwest United States (48%), whereas swine production facilities in the NGP region account for 13% of U.S. pork production and swine facilities in the southeastern United States account for 15% of production (USDA, 2009b). There are inherent differ- ences between NGP and southeastern United States produc- tion operations, with the most important being a substantial increase in heating and ventilation requirements (i.e., higher energy demands) for the NGP region, where supplemental heating is required for 3-4 months of the year. In contrast, the majority of production facilities in the southeastern United States are naturally ventilated (curtain-sided buildings), whereas most facilities in the NGP are mechanically venti- lated (solid sided with fans). Water use is generally higher for the southeastern United States because of increased use of water-based dripper or mister systems for SOW cooling. Completed feed transport distances are generally greater in the NGP because of its larger geographical area requiring

feedstuff producers to use centralized mills to provide dis- persed feed demands. In comparison, the majority of the feedstuff in the southeastern United States is delivered via rail transport to a centralized mill; NGP producers have the ad- vantage of producing their own regional corn commonly transported via overland trucks. Feed mix ratios are generally comparable (80% corn and 20% soybean meal), as are dry distillers grain inclusion rates provided by local ethanol pro- duction facilities. There are considerable differences in manure management practices between these locations, in- cluding the method of storage (under slat storage for NGP compared with lagoon for the southeast) and method of ap- plication (either tanker truck or hose pull injected compared with hose pull with irrigation application for the southeast), although all achieve similar degrees of "treatment" via pas- sive anaerobic processes. Lagoon systems typically result in greater emissions of methane, ammonia, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen sulfide because of a larger exposed surface area, and the fertilizing quality of the manure is reduced because of increased nitrogen volatilization rates, although the loss of manure fertilizing quality may not be as important because of the type of cropland on which the manure is spread (corn and soybeans for the NGP compared with costal Bermuda grass hay in the southeast).

The purpose of this study was to conduct a life cycle as- sessment (LCA) of a theoretical modern U.S. NGP swine production facility to assess the net environmental impacts associated with typical lifespan of a head of swine produced at the facility. The conservative "gate-to-gate" analysis did not account for abattoir or other postproduction supply chain processes, or upstream feedstuff production and associated feedstuff transport to a regional feedmill. Instead, this LCA model focuses solely on feed manufacturing and production facility operations, where the LCA model outcomes provide a process-level understanding of environmental impacts en- countered during typical NGP production facility operations. Although exclusion of many pre- and postproduction pro- cesses prevents a comprehensive evaluation of a complete pork life cycle, the process-level evaluation utilized within this study eliminates many uncertainties associated with feedstuff production and the supply chain processes once the product leaves the production facility "gate" and allows pork producers and environmental practitioners to target "hot spots" (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005) within facility operations to better identify and optimize the life cycle of pork production.

Since 1996, a greater percentage of the annual pig crop has come from farms with >5,000 head (USDA, 2009a). By 2008, 50,700 hog operations had fewer than 99 head of swine and ac- counted for <1% of the U.S. swine inventory, whereas 2,900 operations had >5,000 head and accounted for >61% of the

U.S. swine inventory (USDA, 2009a). Although per capita pork consumption in the United States has not significantly changed since 1960 (22.2kg of boneless equivalent in 1960 compared with 21.1 kg in 2008), there has been a significant growth of the pork export market-by 2008 the United States exported 2,118 metric tons of pork (NPB, 2010). Increased pork production has also resulted in increased demand for products and processes associated with pork production such as feedstuff, water, energy, and manure handling operations. The gradual change toward larger swine production operations has brought much attention to the economic and environmental impacts of pork production. As the size of typical swine production facilities continue to grow, the magnitude of local greenhouse gas (GHG) emis- sions such as manure handling process may also increase; however, these larger facilities tend to operate more effi- ciently, reducing their regional environmental impacts on a per animal basis. Further, the emergence of a consumer

*Corresponding author: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, South Dakota School of Mines and Technology, Rapid City, SD 57701. Phone: +1 394 2443 Fax: +1 605 394 5171; E-mail: james.stone@sdsmt.edu

marketplace demanding sustainable management practices is now requiring the pork industry to better scrutinize their environmental burdens. The carbon footprint associated with U.S. pork production was identified as an emerging concern by the National Pork Board (Stokes, 2009), and this industry is now contentiously implementing methods to minimize GHG emissions and other associated environmental burdens.

There have been several recent studies investigating the carbon footprint and other life cycle-related impacts associ- ated with animal production processes. Most studies have focused on production within the European Union (EU) (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Eriksson et al., 2005; Nunez and Fermoso, 2005; Basset-Mens et al., 2007; Dalgaard et al., 2007; Edwards-Jones et al., 2009) or Australia (Peters et al., 2010; Wiedemann et al., 2010), and only few North American-based studies are currently available. Direct com- parisons between these studies and those presented within this or other North American-based studies can be challeng- ing due to (1) differences in genetic make-up of EU swine herd; (2) utilization of nontraditional (from a U.S. perspective) feedstuff; (3) typically less-efficient ventilation systems for EU facilities; (4) differences in market weight pigs as EU market pigs are generally lighter weight resulting in greater feed ef- ficiency gains; and (5) differences in manure management practices (Brewer et al., 1998). However, there have been a number of important findings from these studies, which have significant relevance for Northern Great Plains (NGP) com- prising of Minnesota, North Dakota, and South Dakota swine production operations. Feed choice, including related enteric emissions (Peters et al., 2010), and feedstuff production (Eriksson et al., 2005) were found to have major contributions toward life cycle environmental impacts. In particular, Eriksson et al. (2005) found that the use of soybean products (commonly referred to as soymeal in the United States) had significantly higher environmental production cost compared with other sources of crude protein such as peas and rapeseed meal. Elimination of soymeal resulted in reducing energy use by 10%, GHG emissions by 7%, and eutrophication impacts by 17%. Dalgaard et al. (2007) reported that feed production and manure handling were the highest contributions to global warming, eutrophication, and acidification-related impacts for Danish pig farming, in which, like NGP facilities, manure slurry is commonly applied to minimize or compliment syn- thetic fertilizer use.

The largest share of U.S. production resides in the Midwest United States (48%), whereas swine production facilities in the NGP region account for 13% of U.S. pork production and swine facilities in the southeastern United States account for 15% of production (USDA, 2009b). There are inherent differ- ences between NGP and southeastern United States produc- tion operations, with the most important being a substantial increase in heating and ventilation requirements (i.e., higher energy demands) for the NGP region, where supplemental heating is required for 3-4 months of the year. In contrast, the majority of production facilities in the southeastern United States are naturally ventilated (curtain-sided buildings), whereas most facilities in the NGP are mechanically venti- lated (solid sided with fans). Water use is generally higher for the southeastern United States because of increased use of water-based dripper or mister systems for SOW cooling. Completed feed transport distances are generally greater in the NGP because of its larger geographical area requiring

feedstuff producers to use centralized mills to provide dis- persed feed demands. In comparison, the majority of the feedstuff in the southeastern United States is delivered via rail transport to a centralized mill; NGP producers have the ad- vantage of producing their own regional corn commonly transported via overland trucks. Feed mix ratios are generally comparable (80% corn and 20% soybean meal), as are dry distillers grain inclusion rates provided by local ethanol pro- duction facilities. There are considerable differences in manure management practices between these locations, in- cluding the method of storage (under slat storage for NGP compared with lagoon for the southeast) and method of ap- plication (either tanker truck or hose pull injected compared with hose pull with irrigation application for the southeast), although all achieve similar degrees of "treatment" via pas- sive anaerobic processes. Lagoon systems typically result in greater emissions of methane, ammonia, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen sulfide because of a larger exposed surface area, and the fertilizing quality of the manure is reduced because of increased nitrogen volatilization rates, although the loss of manure fertilizing quality may not be as important because of the type of cropland on which the manure is spread (corn and soybeans for the NGP compared with costal Bermuda grass hay in the southeast).

The purpose of this study was to conduct a life cycle as- sessment (LCA) of a theoretical modern U.S. NGP swine production facility to assess the net environmental impacts associated with typical lifespan of a head of swine produced at the facility. The conservative "gate-to-gate" analysis did not account for abattoir or other postproduction supply chain processes, or upstream feedstuff production and associated feedstuff transport to a regional feedmill. Instead, this LCA model focuses solely on feed manufacturing and production facility operations, where the LCA model outcomes provide a process-level understanding of environmental impacts en- countered during typical NGP production facility operations. Although exclusion of many pre- and postproduction pro- cesses prevents a comprehensive evaluation of a complete pork life cycle, the process-level evaluation utilized within this study eliminates many uncertainties associated with feedstuff production and the supply chain processes once the product leaves the production facility "gate" and allows pork producers and environmental practitioners to target "hot spots" (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005) within facility operations to better identify and optimize the life cycle of pork production.

10: <header>Materials and Methods </header>
Goal and scope definition

The life cycle impacts associated with four distinct opera- tion scenarios (normal operations, antimicrobial feed additive use, reduced completed feed transport distance, and in- creased renewable energy use) for a theoretical modern NGP swine production facility were evaluated.
Functional unit

The basis of this study was one head of swine produced from postgestation 29kg) to market weight (118 deliv- ered to the exit gate of the production facility. All LCA input data were normalized to this per head of swine unit of mea-

0: sure. Study results using our per head of swine functional unit are comparable to other commonly used functional units, such as kg of pork produced leaving the facility (Basset-Mens and van der Werf, 2005; Eriksson et al., 2005; Edwards-Jones et al., 2009) by dividing our results by net pig growth (89kg), or kg of hot standard carcass (Peters et al., 2010) by dividing our results by net pig growth times 0.74.
1: <header>System boundaries </header>
The system boundaries for this study consisted of the fol- lowing feed manufacturing and production facility opera- tional stages as shown in Fig. 1, including:

<list>Feed manufacturing, including electricity use and com- pleted feed transport to the swine production facility; 
Swine production facility operations, including: 
Manure storage and handling consisting of manure auger operation, manure tank truck operations, and manure pumping for land application; 
Swine enteric emissions; 
Electricity generation and propane production, in- cluding mechanical ventilation, lighting, propane heating, and feed auger operations; and 
Water extraction for cooling, cleaning, and consumption.</list>

The system boundaries do not account for abattoir or other postproduction supply chain processes, or upstream feedstuff production and associated feedstuff transport to a regional feedmill. Instead, this article focuses on a process-level under- standing of environmental impacts associated directly within facility operations, neglecting both pre- and postsupply chain impacts, which we consider to be relatively consistent throughout the United States (slightly different feedstuff ratios are employed at various U.S. regions). It should also be noted that the choice of feedstuff can have a significant influence on most environmental impacts (Eriksson et al., 2005; Peters et al.,

2010), whereas postslaughterhouse transport to the market- place has been reported to have an insignificant contribution (<1%) to the total embodied pork GHG emissions for Danish conditions (Dalgaard et al., 2007).
Inventory analysis

The LCA model was assembled using SimaPro 7.1 LCA modeling software (PRé Consultants). Model input parame- ters were assembled through discussions with professional swine producers, two site visits to regional feedmills and production facilities (3,600 head facility in South Dakota and 4,800 head facility in Minnesota), and consultation of opera- tions reference materials and published operations literature; all of which were used to develop model conditions atypi- cal to NGP swine production operations. The theoretical production facility model was developed to estimate the en- vironmental impacts for this industry under climatic, geo- graphic, and environmental conditions unique to the NGP, and the data was assembled following ISO 14040 standards (ISO, 2006). All model input parameters were compiled using U.S. LCI database values (NREL, 2010a) unless otherwise noted. The model results are intended to provide both swine producers and environmental practitioners with a compre- hensive overview of environmental impacts associated with operations typical for this region.

Uncertainty associated with the impacts was determined by Monte Carlo analysis assuming normal distribution with a variance not exceeding 1.5 for up to 1,000 model runs, as recommended by the SimaPro users manual, and presented as 95% confidence interval associated with mean impact value. A random value was chosen for each network flow, with the resulting range of all calculations providing a specific degree of uncertainty.
The data used to calculate the energy, transportation, and environmental variables per functional unit were collected

System Boundary

FIG. 1. System boundary for the Northern Great Plains swine production facility life cycle assessment model. Life cycle-associated impacts for feedstuff production and transportation and postproduc- tion processes (abattoir and distribution within consumer marketplace) were not included within the system boundaries. Specific process stages modeled (highlighted in gray) include [1] feed manufacturing; and swine production facility operations, including [2] manure storage and handling; [3] enteric emissions; [4] electricity gener- ation and propane production; and [5] water extraction.

through on-site visits and interviews with regional swine producers as discussed above. Below are specific operations input data and assumptions used within the LCA model de- velopment, presented in numerical order as shown within the system boundaries (Fig. 1). Incidental common use products found within production facility operations, such as laundry detergents, cleaning supplies, disinfectants, and antibiotics or antimicrobials, were not accounted for within the model input data. With the exception of antimicrobials discussed within the scenario analysis, incidental use was assumed to have an insignificant contribution to environmental impacts when compared with the primary production processes evaluated within the model. Building infrastructure materials were also not accounted for. South Dakota energy portfolio used for feed manufacturing and swine production facility operations is shown in Table 1 (US-EIA, 2010).

<list>1. Feed manufacturing: Throughout a typical pig's life cycle, it was assumed that 276kg of feed was con- sumed, consisting of 80% corn and 20% soymeal, typi- cal of NGP operations (DeRouchey et al., 2007). Energy to operate the mill was assumed to be the only signifi- cant input for this process stage. The energy required to mill 1 ton of feed was normalized to per head of swine life cycle, based upon energy requirements for two feed mills visited (Table 2). Completed feed was assumed shipped from Dell Rapids, SD, to a hypothetical pro- duction facility located in Nunda, SD, at a travel dis- tance of 64.4km This shipping distance is typical for the NGP, where central feedmills provide completed feed to many regional animal production industries. A 16-ton-capacity diesel truck was assumed operated at 50% load capacity (empty return travel). 
2. Swine production facility-manure storage and han- dling: 
Manure emissions: For a modern NGP production facility, manure is typically collected and stored in >4,000,000-L (1,300 to 1,800 m² surface area) under- ground lined holding tanks housed directly beneath the facility, commonly referred to as pit recharge or underslat flush gutter designs (Jones and Eldridge, 2008). Emissions associated with manure storage were based upon fugitive emission rates per nominal sur- face area (ca. ug/[s m²]) determined by Lague et al. (2005) and Lim et al. (2003). Emission estimates for this</list>

study were normalized to an assumed storage surface area (1,326 m2 and duration (365 days storage) for carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ammonia, methane, and hydrogen sulfide metabolic emissions (Table 3).

Manure land application and transport: Environ- mental impacts associated with manure land appli- cation were attributed to the production facility operations instead of benefiting feedstuff production (i.e., offsetting synthetic fertilizer requirements), in- cluding postmanure application nutrient loading im- pacts. Manure is typically pumped directly from the holding tanks when land applied using either an umbilical cord pumping arrangement, in which ma- nure is pumped using either a centrifugal or positive displacement pump arrangement through a long hose to neighboring fields (Gilbertson et al., 2008), or pumped to a tank truck and transported to a local manure application spreader or injector. The manure land application energy requirements were deter- mined by assuming that half of the manure volume was land applied via tank truck and spreader, and the other half was pumped and applied directly from the storage pit using a submersible pump, 1,100-m-long umbilical cord pipeline, and a "big gun" applicator, which directly injects manure. NGP production facil- ities typically use one if not both of these application methods, and SO a 50% use rate was assumed to be a conservative estimate that accounted for both process impacts. Ventilation stage|Additional blower fan power (W)|Total blower fan power (W)|Operation time (days) 1|186|186|160 2|249|435|128 3|249|684|112 4|373|1,057|32 5|746|1,803|8 6|746|2,549|5 7|746|3,295|2
Values presented are considered typical for a modern NGP U.S. production facility and are based upon the average of two NGP production facility operations.

<list>tion. Stages 1 through 7 represent a sequential series of blower operations from the least degree of venti- lation (stage 1 using a single 186 W fan) to stage 7 providing the greatest ventilation (seven fans operat- ing simultaneously). The assumed facility consisted of four rooms that each housed 1,200 head of swine, for a total of 4,800 head of swine contained within the fa- cility at a given time. Each room of the facility was assumed equipped with the seven-stage ventilation system, allowing power requirements to be normal- ized per head of swine. For the 160-day expected swine life cycle (i.e., average time one head of swine was housed within the facility), an estimated total ventilation operation running time for each ventila- tion stage was determined for ventilation energy (Table 4). The total energy required for ventilation was determined by the operations time (seconds) and energy consumed (watts) and was normalized per head of swine. 
Lighting: Lighting energy demands (for both building lighting and heat lamps for space heating) were de- termined in a similar manner as ventilation. Each of the four facility rooms were assumed to have 30 light banks consisting of 23 W fluorescent bulbs, each op- erating 2h/day (Table 2). 
Heating: A typical NGP U.S. swine production facility is heated using multiple propane 60,000 BTU blower heaters. Typical propane use characteristics were re- viewed at two NGP production facilities, and esti- mates of annual propane consumption use were determined assuming each gallon of propane was assumed to provide 91,500 BTUs of thermal heat, with an expected 80% efficiency resulting in 73,200 BTUs available (Table 2). 
Feed auger operations: The energy requirements as- sociated with feed auger operations were determined by the assumed number of operational augers (eight in the facility, with two housed within each of the four facility rooms), the power requirement of each auger (746W/auger), and the daily time of operation (0.75h/day) (Table 2). 
5. Swine production facility-water extraction: Water consumption estimates (908.4kg water per head of swine) were determined based upon the specific swine age requirements and life cycle weight gains (Barry Kerkeart, Pipestone Systems, pers. comm.). Water con- sumption included 12.617kg water per head of swine, required for production facility operations cleaning and washdown (Table 1). Swine production facilities located in the NGP typically rely on either chlorinated or un- treated groundwater as their primary water source. Chlorine use was not accounted for within the model.</list>
2: <header>System boundaries </header>
The system boundaries for this study consisted of the fol- lowing feed manufacturing and production facility opera- tional stages as shown in Fig. 1, including:

<list>Feed manufacturing, including electricity use and com- pleted feed transport to the swine production facility; 
Swine production facility operations, including: 
Manure storage and handling consisting of manure auger operation, manure tank truck operations, and manure pumping for land application; 
Swine enteric emissions; 
Electricity generation and propane production, in- cluding mechanical ventilation, lighting, propane heating, and feed auger operations; and 
Water extraction for cooling, cleaning, and consumption.</list>

The system boundaries do not account for abattoir or other postproduction supply chain processes, or upstream feedstuff production and associated feedstuff transport to a regional feedmill. Instead, this article focuses on a process-level under- standing of environmental impacts associated directly within facility operations, neglecting both pre- and postsupply chain impacts, which we consider to be relatively consistent throughout the United States (slightly different feedstuff ratios are employed at various U.S. regions). It should also be noted that the choice of feedstuff can have a significant influence on most environmental impacts (Eriksson et al., 2005; Peters et al.,

2010), whereas postslaughterhouse transport to the market- place has been reported to have an insignificant contribution (<1%) to the total embodied pork GHG emissions for Danish conditions (Dalgaard et al., 2007).
Inventory analysis

The LCA model was assembled using SimaPro 7.1 LCA modeling software (PRé Consultants). Model input parame- ters were assembled through discussions with professional swine producers, two site visits to regional feedmills and production facilities (3,600 head facility in South Dakota and 4,800 head facility in Minnesota), and consultation of opera- tions reference materials and published operations literature; all of which were used to develop model conditions atypi- cal to NGP swine production operations. The theoretical production facility model was developed to estimate the en- vironmental impacts for this industry under climatic, geo- graphic, and environmental conditions unique to the NGP, and the data was assembled following ISO 14040 standards (ISO, 2006). All model input parameters were compiled using U.S. LCI database values (NREL, 2010a) unless otherwise noted. The model results are intended to provide both swine producers and environmental practitioners with a compre- hensive overview of environmental impacts associated with operations typical for this region.

Uncertainty associated with the impacts was determined by Monte Carlo analysis assuming normal distribution with a variance not exceeding 1.5 for up to 1,000 model runs, as recommended by the SimaPro users manual, and presented as 95% confidence interval associated with mean impact value. A random value was chosen for each network flow, with the resulting range of all calculations providing a specific degree of uncertainty.

FIG. 1. System boundary for the Northern Great Plains swine production facility life cycle assessment model. Life cycle-associated impacts for feedstuff production and transportation and postproduc- tion processes (abattoir and distribution within consumer marketplace) were not included within the system boundaries. Specific process stages modeled (highlighted in gray) include [1] feed manufacturing; and swine production facility operations, including [2] manure storage and handling; [3] enteric emissions; [4] electricity gener- ation and propane production; and [5] water extraction.

through on-site visits and interviews with regional swine producers as discussed above. Below are specific operations input data and assumptions used within the LCA model de- velopment, presented in numerical order as shown within the system boundaries (Fig. 1). Incidental common use products found within production facility operations, such as laundry detergents, cleaning supplies, disinfectants, and antibiotics or antimicrobials, were not accounted for within the model input data. With the exception of antimicrobials discussed within the scenario analysis, incidental use was assumed to have an insignificant contribution to environmental impacts when compared with the primary production processes evaluated within the model. Building infrastructure materials were also not accounted for. South Dakota energy portfolio used for feed manufacturing and swine production facility operations is shown in Table 1 (US-EIA, 2010).

<list>1. Feed manufacturing: Throughout a typical pig's life cycle, it was assumed that 276kg of feed was con- sumed, consisting of 80% corn and 20% soymeal, typi- cal of NGP operations (DeRouchey et al., 2007). Energy to operate the mill was assumed to be the only signifi- cant input for this process stage. The energy required to mill 1 ton of feed was normalized to per head of swine life cycle, based upon energy requirements for two feed mills visited (Table 2). Completed feed was assumed shipped from Dell Rapids, SD, to a hypothetical pro- duction facility located in Nunda, SD, at a travel dis- tance of 64.4km This shipping distance is typical for the NGP, where central feedmills provide completed feed to many regional animal production industries. A 16-ton-capacity diesel truck was assumed operated at 50% load capacity (empty return travel). 
2. Swine production facility-manure storage and han- dling: 
Manure emissions: For a modern NGP production facility, manure is typically collected and stored in >4,000,000-L (1,300 to 1,800 m² surface area) under- ground lined holding tanks housed directly beneath the facility, commonly referred to as pit recharge or underslat flush gutter designs (Jones and Eldridge, 2008). Emissions associated with manure storage were based upon fugitive emission rates per nominal sur- face area (ca. ug/[s m²]) determined by Lague et al. (2005) and Lim et al. (2003). Emission estimates for this</list>

study were normalized to an assumed storage surface area (1,326 m2 and duration (365 days storage) for carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ammonia, methane, and hydrogen sulfide metabolic emissions (Table 3).

Manure land application and transport: Environ- mental impacts associated with manure land appli- cation were attributed to the production facility operations instead of benefiting feedstuff production (i.e., offsetting synthetic fertilizer requirements), in- cluding postmanure application nutrient loading im- pacts. Manure is typically pumped directly from the holding tanks when land applied using either an umbilical cord pumping arrangement, in which ma- nure is pumped using either a centrifugal or positive displacement pump arrangement through a long hose to neighboring fields (Gilbertson et al., 2008), or pumped to a tank truck and transported to a local manure application spreader or injector. The manure land application energy requirements were deter- mined by assuming that half of the manure volume was land applied via tank truck and spreader, and the other half was pumped and applied directly from the storage pit using a submersible pump, 1,100-m-long umbilical cord pipeline, and a "big gun" applicator, which directly injects manure. NGP production facil- ities typically use one if not both of these application methods, and SO a 50% use rate was assumed to be a conservative estimate that accounted for both process impacts. Below are specific operations input data and assumptions used within the LCA model de- velopment, presented in numerical order as shown within the system boundaries (Fig. 1). Incidental common use products found within production facility operations, such as laundry detergents, cleaning supplies, disinfectants, and antibiotics or antimicrobials, were not accounted for within the model input data. With the exception of antimicrobials discussed within the scenario analysis, incidental use was assumed to have an insignificant contribution to environmental impacts when compared with the primary production processes evaluated within the model. Building infrastructure materials were also not accounted for. South Dakota energy portfolio used for feed manufacturing and swine production facility operations is shown in Table 1 (US-EIA, 2010).

<list>1. Feed manufacturing: Throughout a typical pig's life cycle, it was assumed that 276kg of feed was con- sumed, consisting of 80% corn and 20% soymeal, typi- cal of NGP operations (DeRouchey et al., 2007). Energy to operate the mill was assumed to be the only signifi- cant input for this process stage. The energy required to mill 1 ton of feed was normalized to per head of swine life cycle, based upon energy requirements for two feed mills visited (Table 2). Completed feed was assumed shipped from Dell Rapids, SD, to a hypothetical pro- duction facility located in Nunda, SD, at a travel dis- tance of 64.4km This shipping distance is typical for the NGP, where central feedmills provide completed feed to many regional animal production industries. A 16-ton-capacity diesel truck was assumed operated at 50% load capacity (empty return travel). 
2. Swine production facility-manure storage and han- dling: 
Manure emissions: For a modern NGP production facility, manure is typically collected and stored in >4,000,000-L (1,300 to 1,800 m² surface area) under- ground lined holding tanks housed directly beneath the facility, commonly referred to as pit recharge or underslat flush gutter designs (Jones and Eldridge, 2008). Emissions associated with manure storage were based upon fugitive emission rates per nominal sur- face area (ca. ug/[s m²]) determined by Lague et al. (2005) and Lim et al. (2003). Emission estimates for this</list>

study were normalized to an assumed storage surface area (1,326 m2 and duration (365 days storage) for carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ammonia, methane, and hydrogen sulfide metabolic emissions (Table 3).

Manure land application and transport: Environ- mental impacts associated with manure land appli- cation were attributed to the production facility operations instead of benefiting feedstuff production (i.e., offsetting synthetic fertilizer requirements), in- cluding postmanure application nutrient loading im- pacts. Manure is typically pumped directly from the holding tanks when land applied using either an umbilical cord pumping arrangement, in which ma- nure is pumped using either a centrifugal or positive displacement pump arrangement through a long hose to neighboring fields (Gilbertson et al., 2008), or pumped to a tank truck and transported to a local manure application spreader or injector. The manure land application energy requirements were deter- mined by assuming that half of the manure volume was land applied via tank truck and spreader, and the other half was pumped and applied directly from the storage pit using a submersible pump, 1,100-m-long umbilical cord pipeline, and a "big gun" applicator, which directly injects manure. NGP production facil- ities typically use one if not both of these application methods, and SO a 50% use rate was assumed to be a conservative estimate that accounted for both process impacts. For the truck and spreader method, transport distance was assumed 8 km, and the manure mass was determined by estimating the manure bulk density (1.0255kg/L and the volume of the manure transported each year (1,510,872 L). Pumping requirements were accomplished using 74.6kW diesel pump running for 5.26h/year using 294.19k] energy required per head. For land applica- tion pumping using the umbilical cord hosing and big gun applicator, pumping power requirement was 22.3 kW and operation time was 8.84h/cycle for one cycle (Table 2). 
3. Swine production facility-enteric emissions: Swine enteric emissions, based upon the study by Harper et al. (2004), for carbon dioxide, methane, and ammonia are presented in Table 3. 
4. Swine production facility-electricity generation and propane production: 
Mechanical ventilation: A modern NGP swine pro- duction facility typically employs mechanical venti- lation using a temperature actuated, seven-stage ventilation system with temperature set-points and blower use based upon the desired operation climatic conditions required inside the facility (Hoff, 2008). These operations contrast to southeastern U.S. facili- ties, which employ sheet curtains for natural ventila-</list>
TABLE 4. SWINE PRODUCTION FACILITY MECHANICAL VENTILATION STAGES AND POWER REQUIREMENT MODEL INPUT DATA
Ventilation stage|Additional blower fan power (W)|Total blower fan power (W)|Operation time (days) 1|186|186|160 2|249|435|128 3|249|684|112 4|373|1,057|32 5|746|1,803|8 6|746|2,549|5 7|746|3,295|2
Values presented are considered typical for a modern NGP U.S. production facility and are based upon the average of two NGP production facility operations.

<list>tion. Stages 1 through 7 represent a sequential series of blower operations from the least degree of venti- lation (stage 1 using a single 186 W fan) to stage 7 providing the greatest ventilation (seven fans operat- ing simultaneously). The assumed facility consisted of four rooms that each housed 1,200 head of swine, for a total of 4,800 head of swine contained within the fa- cility at a given time. Each room of the facility was assumed equipped with the seven-stage ventilation system, allowing power requirements to be normal- ized per head of swine. For the 160-day expected swine life cycle (i.e., average time one head of swine was housed within the facility), an estimated total ventilation operation running time for each ventila- tion stage was determined for ventilation energy (Table 4). The total energy required for ventilation was determined by the operations time (seconds) and energy consumed (watts) and was normalized per head of swine. 
Lighting: Lighting energy demands (for both building lighting and heat lamps for space heating) were de- termined in a similar manner as ventilation. Each of the four facility rooms were assumed to have 30 light banks consisting of 23 W fluorescent bulbs, each op- erating 2h/day (Table 2). 
Heating: A typical NGP U.S. swine production facility is heated using multiple propane 60,000 BTU blower heaters. Typical propane use characteristics were re- viewed at two NGP production facilities, and esti- mates of annual propane consumption use were determined assuming each gallon of propane was assumed to provide 91,500 BTUs of thermal heat, with an expected 80% efficiency resulting in 73,200 BTUs available (Table 2). 
Feed auger operations: The energy requirements as- sociated with feed auger operations were determined by the assumed number of operational augers (eight in the facility, with two housed within each of the four facility rooms), the power requirement of each auger (746W/auger), and the daily time of operation (0.75h/day) (Table 2). 
5. Swine production facility-water extraction: Water consumption estimates (908.4kg water per head of swine) were determined based upon the specific swine age requirements and life cycle weight gains (Barry Kerkeart, Pipestone Systems, pers. comm.). Water con- sumption included 12.617kg water per head of swine, required for production facility operations cleaning and washdown (Table 1). Swine production facilities located in the NGP typically rely on either chlorinated or un- treated groundwater as their primary water source. Chlorine use was not accounted for within the model.</list>
5: <header>System boundaries </header>
The system boundaries for this study consisted of the fol- lowing feed manufacturing and production facility opera- tional stages as shown in Fig. 1, including:

<list>Feed manufacturing, including electricity use and com- pleted feed transport to the swine production facility; 
Swine production facility operations, including: 
Manure storage and handling consisting of manure auger operation, manure tank truck operations, and manure pumping for land application; 
Swine enteric emissions; 
Electricity generation and propane production, in- cluding mechanical ventilation, lighting, propane heating, and feed auger operations; and 
Water extraction for cooling, cleaning, and consumption.</list>

The system boundaries do not account for abattoir or other postproduction supply chain processes, or upstream feedstuff production and associated feedstuff transport to a regional feedmill. Instead, this article focuses on a process-level under- standing of environmental impacts associated directly within facility operations, neglecting both pre- and postsupply chain impacts, which we consider to be relatively consistent throughout the United States (slightly different feedstuff ratios are employed at various U.S. regions). It should also be noted that the choice of feedstuff can have a significant influence on most environmental impacts (Eriksson et al., 2005; Peters et al.,

2010), whereas postslaughterhouse transport to the market- place has been reported to have an insignificant contribution (<1%) to the total embodied pork GHG emissions for Danish conditions (Dalgaard et al., 2007).
Inventory analysis

The LCA model was assembled using SimaPro 7.1 LCA modeling software (PRé Consultants). Model input parame- ters were assembled through discussions with professional swine producers, two site visits to regional feedmills and production facilities (3,600 head facility in South Dakota and 4,800 head facility in Minnesota), and consultation of opera- tions reference materials and published operations literature; all of which were used to develop model conditions atypi- cal to NGP swine production operations. The theoretical production facility model was developed to estimate the en- vironmental impacts for this industry under climatic, geo- graphic, and environmental conditions unique to the NGP, and the data was assembled following ISO 14040 standards (ISO, 2006). All model input parameters were compiled using U.S. LCI database values (NREL, 2010a) unless otherwise noted. The model results are intended to provide both swine producers and environmental practitioners with a compre- hensive overview of environmental impacts associated with operations typical for this region.

Uncertainty associated with the impacts was determined by Monte Carlo analysis assuming normal distribution with a variance not exceeding 1.5 for up to 1,000 model runs, as recommended by the SimaPro users manual, and presented as 95% confidence interval associated with mean impact value. A random value was chosen for each network flow, with the resulting range of all calculations providing a specific degree of uncertainty.

3. Swine production facility-enteric emissions: Swine enteric emissions, based upon the study by Harper et al. (2004), for carbon dioxide, methane, and ammonia are presented in Table 3. 
4. Swine production facility-electricity generation and propane production: 
Mechanical ventilation: A modern NGP swine pro- duction facility typically employs mechanical venti- lation using a temperature actuated, seven-stage ventilation system with temperature set-points and blower use based upon the desired operation climatic conditions required inside the facility (Hoff, 2008). These operations contrast to southeastern U.S. facili- ties, which employ sheet curtains for natural ventila-</list>
TABLE 4. SWINE PRODUCTION FACILITY MECHANICAL VENTILATION STAGES AND POWER REQUIREMENT MODEL INPUT DATA
Ventilation stage|Additional blower fan power (W)|Total blower fan power (W)|Operation time (days) 1|186|186|160 2|249|435|128 3|249|684|112 4|373|1,057|32 5|746|1,803|8 6|746|2,549|5 7|746|3,295|2
Values presented are considered typical for a modern NGP U.S. production facility and are based upon the average of two NGP production facility operations.

<list>tion. Stages 1 through 7 represent a sequential series of blower operations from the least degree of venti- lation (stage 1 using a single 186 W fan) to stage 7 providing the greatest ventilation (seven fans operat- ing simultaneously). The assumed facility consisted of four rooms that each housed 1,200 head of swine, for a total of 4,800 head of swine contained within the fa- cility at a given time. Each room of the facility was assumed equipped with the seven-stage ventilation system, allowing power requirements to be normal- ized per head of swine. For the 160-day expected swine life cycle (i.e., average time one head of swine was housed within the facility), an estimated total ventilation operation running time for each ventila- tion stage was determined for ventilation energy (Table 4). The total energy required for ventilation was determined by the operations time (seconds) and energy consumed (watts) and was normalized per head of swine. 
Lighting: Lighting energy demands (for both building lighting and heat lamps for space heating) were de- termined in a similar manner as ventilation. Each of the four facility rooms were assumed to have 30 light banks consisting of 23 W fluorescent bulbs, each op- erating 2h/day (Table 2). 
Heating: A typical NGP U.S. swine production facility is heated using multiple propane 60,000 BTU blower heaters. Typical propane use characteristics were re- viewed at two NGP production facilities, and esti- mates of annual propane consumption use were determined assuming each gallon of propane was assumed to provide 91,500 BTUs of thermal heat, with an expected 80% efficiency resulting in 73,200 BTUs available (Table 2). 
Feed auger operations: The energy requirements as- sociated with feed auger operations were determined by the assumed number of operational augers (eight in the facility, with two housed within each of the four facility rooms), the power requirement of each auger (746W/auger), and the daily time of operation (0.75h/day) (Table 2). 
5. Swine production facility-water extraction: Water consumption estimates (908.4kg water per head of swine) were determined based upon the specific swine age requirements and life cycle weight gains (Barry Kerkeart, Pipestone Systems, pers. comm.). Water con- sumption included 12.617kg water per head of swine, required for production facility operations cleaning and washdown (Table 1). Swine production facilities located in the NGP typically rely on either chlorinated or un- treated groundwater as their primary water source. Chlorine use was not accounted for within the model.</list>
7: <header>System boundaries </header>
The system boundaries for this study consisted of the fol- lowing feed manufacturing and production facility opera- tional stages as shown in Fig. 1, including:

<list>Feed manufacturing, including electricity use and com- pleted feed transport to the swine production facility; 
Swine production facility operations, including: 
Manure storage and handling consisting of manure auger operation, manure tank truck operations, and manure pumping for land application; 
Swine enteric emissions; 
Electricity generation and propane production, in- cluding mechanical ventilation, lighting, propane heating, and feed auger operations; and 
Water extraction for cooling, cleaning, and consumption.</list>

The system boundaries do not account for abattoir or other postproduction supply chain processes, or upstream feedstuff production and associated feedstuff transport to a regional feedmill. Instead, this article focuses on a process-level under- standing of environmental impacts associated directly within facility operations, neglecting both pre- and postsupply chain impacts, which we consider to be relatively consistent throughout the United States (slightly different feedstuff ratios are employed at various U.S. regions). It should also be noted that the choice of feedstuff can have a significant influence on most environmental impacts (Eriksson et al., 2005; Peters et al.,

2010), whereas postslaughterhouse transport to the market- place has been reported to have an insignificant contribution (<1%) to the total embodied pork GHG emissions for Danish conditions (Dalgaard et al., 2007).
Inventory analysis

The LCA model was assembled using SimaPro 7.1 LCA modeling software (PRé Consultants). Model input parame- ters were assembled through discussions with professional swine producers, two site visits to regional feedmills and production facilities (3,600 head facility in South Dakota and 4,800 head facility in Minnesota), and consultation of opera- tions reference materials and published operations literature; all of which were used to develop model conditions atypi- cal to NGP swine production operations. The theoretical production facility model was developed to estimate the en- vironmental impacts for this industry under climatic, geo- graphic, and environmental conditions unique to the NGP, and the data was assembled following ISO 14040 standards (ISO, 2006). All model input parameters were compiled using U.S. LCI database values (NREL, 2010a) unless otherwise noted. The model results are intended to provide both swine producers and environmental practitioners with a compre- hensive overview of environmental impacts associated with operations typical for this region.

Uncertainty associated with the impacts was determined by Monte Carlo analysis assuming normal distribution with a variance not exceeding 1.5 for up to 1,000 model runs, as recommended by the SimaPro users manual, and presented as 95% confidence interval associated with mean impact value. A random value was chosen for each network flow, with the resulting range of all calculations providing a specific degree of uncertainty.
The data used to calculate the energy, transportation, and environmental variables per functional unit were collected

FIG. 1. System boundary for the Northern Great Plains swine production facility life cycle assessment model. Life cycle-associated impacts for feedstuff production and transportation and postproduc- tion processes (abattoir and distribution within consumer marketplace) were not included within the system boundaries. Specific process stages modeled (highlighted in gray) include [1] feed manufacturing; and swine production facility operations, including [2] manure storage and handling; [3] enteric emissions; [4] electricity gener- ation and propane production; and [5] water extraction.

through on-site visits and interviews with regional swine producers as discussed above. Below are specific operations input data and assumptions used within the LCA model de- velopment, presented in numerical order as shown within the system boundaries (Fig. 1). Incidental common use products found within production facility operations, such as laundry detergents, cleaning supplies, disinfectants, and antibiotics or antimicrobials, were not accounted for within the model input data. With the exception of antimicrobials discussed within the scenario analysis, incidental use was assumed to have an insignificant contribution to environmental impacts when compared with the primary production processes evaluated within the model. Building infrastructure materials were also not accounted for. South Dakota energy portfolio used for feed manufacturing and swine production facility operations is shown in Table 1 (US-EIA, 2010).

<list>1. Feed manufacturing: Throughout a typical pig's life cycle, it was assumed that 276kg of feed was con- sumed, consisting of 80% corn and 20% soymeal, typi- cal of NGP operations (DeRouchey et al., 2007). Energy to operate the mill was assumed to be the only signifi- cant input for this process stage. The energy required to mill 1 ton of feed was normalized to per head of swine life cycle, based upon energy requirements for two feed mills visited (Table 2). Completed feed was assumed shipped from Dell Rapids, SD, to a hypothetical pro- duction facility located in Nunda, SD, at a travel dis- tance of 64.4km This shipping distance is typical for the NGP, where central feedmills provide completed feed to many regional animal production industries. A 16-ton-capacity diesel truck was assumed operated at 50% load capacity (empty return travel). 
2. Swine production facility-manure storage and han- dling: 
Manure emissions: For a modern NGP production facility, manure is typically collected and stored in >4,000,000-L (1,300 to 1,800 m² surface area) under- ground lined holding tanks housed directly beneath the facility, commonly referred to as pit recharge or underslat flush gutter designs (Jones and Eldridge, 2008). Emissions associated with manure storage were based upon fugitive emission rates per nominal sur- face area (ca. ug/[s m²]) determined by Lague et al. (2005) and Lim et al. (2003). Emission estimates for this</list>

study were normalized to an assumed storage surface area (1,326 m2 and duration (365 days storage) for carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ammonia, methane, and hydrogen sulfide metabolic emissions (Table 3).

Manure land application and transport: Environ- mental impacts associated with manure land appli- cation were attributed to the production facility operations instead of benefiting feedstuff production (i.e., offsetting synthetic fertilizer requirements), in- cluding postmanure application nutrient loading im- pacts. Manure is typically pumped directly from the holding tanks when land applied using either an umbilical cord pumping arrangement, in which ma- nure is pumped using either a centrifugal or positive displacement pump arrangement through a long hose to neighboring fields (Gilbertson et al., 2008), or pumped to a tank truck and transported to a local manure application spreader or injector. The manure land application energy requirements were deter- mined by assuming that half of the manure volume was land applied via tank truck and spreader, and the other half was pumped and applied directly from the storage pit using a submersible pump, 1,100-m-long umbilical cord pipeline, and a "big gun" applicator, which directly injects manure. NGP production facil- ities typically use one if not both of these application methods, and SO a 50% use rate was assumed to be a conservative estimate that accounted for both process impacts. Uncertainty associated with the impacts was determined by Monte Carlo analysis assuming normal distribution with a variance not exceeding 1.5 for up to 1,000 model runs, as recommended by the SimaPro users manual, and presented as 95% confidence interval associated with mean impact value. A random value was chosen for each network flow, with the resulting range of all calculations providing a specific degree of uncertainty.

System Boundary

FIG. 1. System boundary for the Northern Great Plains swine production facility life cycle assessment model. Life cycle-associated impacts for feedstuff production and transportation and postproduc- tion processes (abattoir and distribution within consumer marketplace) were not included within the system boundaries. Specific process stages modeled (highlighted in gray) include [1] feed manufacturing; and swine production facility operations, including [2] manure storage and handling; [3] enteric emissions; [4] electricity gener- ation and propane production; and [5] water extraction.

through on-site visits and interviews with regional swine producers as discussed above. Below are specific operations input data and assumptions used within the LCA model de- velopment, presented in numerical order as shown within the system boundaries (Fig. 1). Incidental common use products found within production facility operations, such as laundry detergents, cleaning supplies, disinfectants, and antibiotics or antimicrobials, were not accounted for within the model input data. With the exception of antimicrobials discussed within the scenario analysis, incidental use was assumed to have an insignificant contribution to environmental impacts when compared with the primary production processes evaluated within the model. Building infrastructure materials were also not accounted for. South Dakota energy portfolio used for feed manufacturing and swine production facility operations is shown in Table 1 (US-EIA, 2010).

<list>1. Feed manufacturing: Throughout a typical pig's life cycle, it was assumed that 276kg of feed was con- sumed, consisting of 80% corn and 20% soymeal, typi- cal of NGP operations (DeRouchey et al., 2007). Energy to operate the mill was assumed to be the only signifi- cant input for this process stage. The energy required to mill 1 ton of feed was normalized to per head of swine life cycle, based upon energy requirements for two feed mills visited (Table 2). Completed feed was assumed shipped from Dell Rapids, SD, to a hypothetical pro- duction facility located in Nunda, SD, at a travel dis- tance of 64.4km This shipping distance is typical for the NGP, where central feedmills provide completed feed to many regional animal production industries. A 16-ton-capacity diesel truck was assumed operated at 50% load capacity (empty return travel). 
2. Swine production facility-manure storage and han- dling: 
Manure emissions: For a modern NGP production facility, manure is typically collected and stored in >4,000,000-L (1,300 to 1,800 m² surface area) under- ground lined holding tanks housed directly beneath the facility, commonly referred to as pit recharge or underslat flush gutter designs (Jones and Eldridge, 2008). Emissions associated with manure storage were based upon fugitive emission rates per nominal sur- face area (ca. ug/[s m²]) determined by Lague et al. (2005) and Lim et al. (2003). Emission estimates for this</list>

study were normalized to an assumed storage surface area (1,326 m2 and duration (365 days storage) for carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ammonia, methane, and hydrogen sulfide metabolic emissions (Table 3).

Manure land application and transport: Environ- mental impacts associated with manure land appli- cation were attributed to the production facility operations instead of benefiting feedstuff production (i.e., offsetting synthetic fertilizer requirements), in- cluding postmanure application nutrient loading im- pacts. Manure is typically pumped directly from the holding tanks when land applied using either an umbilical cord pumping arrangement, in which ma- nure is pumped using either a centrifugal or positive displacement pump arrangement through a long hose to neighboring fields (Gilbertson et al., 2008), or pumped to a tank truck and transported to a local manure application spreader or injector. The manure land application energy requirements were deter- mined by assuming that half of the manure volume was land applied via tank truck and spreader, and the other half was pumped and applied directly from the storage pit using a submersible pump, 1,100-m-long umbilical cord pipeline, and a "big gun" applicator, which directly injects manure. NGP production facil- ities typically use one if not both of these application methods, and SO a 50% use rate was assumed to be a conservative estimate that accounted for both process impacts. <list>tion. Stages 1 through 7 represent a sequential series of blower operations from the least degree of venti- lation (stage 1 using a single 186 W fan) to stage 7 providing the greatest ventilation (seven fans operat- ing simultaneously). The assumed facility consisted of four rooms that each housed 1,200 head of swine, for a total of 4,800 head of swine contained within the fa- cility at a given time. Each room of the facility was assumed equipped with the seven-stage ventilation system, allowing power requirements to be normal- ized per head of swine. For the 160-day expected swine life cycle (i.e., average time one head of swine was housed within the facility), an estimated total ventilation operation running time for each ventila- tion stage was determined for ventilation energy (Table 4). The total energy required for ventilation was determined by the operations time (seconds) and energy consumed (watts) and was normalized per head of swine. 
Lighting: Lighting energy demands (for both building lighting and heat lamps for space heating) were de- termined in a similar manner as ventilation. Each of the four facility rooms were assumed to have 30 light banks consisting of 23 W fluorescent bulbs, each op- erating 2h/day (Table 2). 
Heating: A typical NGP U.S. swine production facility is heated using multiple propane 60,000 BTU blower heaters. Typical propane use characteristics were re- viewed at two NGP production facilities, and esti- mates of annual propane consumption use were determined assuming each gallon of propane was assumed to provide 91,500 BTUs of thermal heat, with an expected 80% efficiency resulting in 73,200 BTUs available (Table 2). 
Feed auger operations: The energy requirements as- sociated with feed auger operations were determined by the assumed number of operational augers (eight in the facility, with two housed within each of the four facility rooms), the power requirement of each auger (746W/auger), and the daily time of operation (0.75h/day) (Table 2). 
5. Swine production facility-water extraction: Water consumption estimates (908.4kg water per head of swine) were determined based upon the specific swine age requirements and life cycle weight gains (Barry Kerkeart, Pipestone Systems, pers. comm.). Water con- sumption included 12.617kg water per head of swine, required for production facility operations cleaning and washdown (Table 1). Swine production facilities located in the NGP typically rely on either chlorinated or un- treated groundwater as their primary water source. Chlorine use was not accounted for within the model.</list>
10: <header>System boundaries </header>
The system boundaries for this study consisted of the fol- lowing feed manufacturing and production facility opera- tional stages as shown in Fig. 1, including:

<list>Feed manufacturing, including electricity use and com- pleted feed transport to the swine production facility; 
Swine production facility operations, including: 
Manure storage and handling consisting of manure auger operation, manure tank truck operations, and manure pumping for land application; 
Swine enteric emissions; 
Electricity generation and propane production, in- cluding mechanical ventilation, lighting, propane heating, and feed auger operations; and 
Water extraction for cooling, cleaning, and consumption.</list>

The system boundaries do not account for abattoir or other postproduction supply chain processes, or upstream feedstuff production and associated feedstuff transport to a regional feedmill. Instead, this article focuses on a process-level under- standing of environmental impacts associated directly within facility operations, neglecting both pre- and postsupply chain impacts, which we consider to be relatively consistent throughout the United States (slightly different feedstuff ratios are employed at various U.S. regions). It should also be noted that the choice of feedstuff can have a significant influence on most environmental impacts (Eriksson et al., 2005; Peters et al.,

2010), whereas postslaughterhouse transport to the market- place has been reported to have an insignificant contribution (<1%) to the total embodied pork GHG emissions for Danish conditions (Dalgaard et al., 2007).
Inventory analysis

The LCA model was assembled using SimaPro 7.1 LCA modeling software (PRé Consultants). Model input parame- ters were assembled through discussions with professional swine producers, two site visits to regional feedmills and production facilities (3,600 head facility in South Dakota and 4,800 head facility in Minnesota), and consultation of opera- tions reference materials and published operations literature; all of which were used to develop model conditions atypi- cal to NGP swine production operations. The theoretical production facility model was developed to estimate the en- vironmental impacts for this industry under climatic, geo- graphic, and environmental conditions unique to the NGP, and the data was assembled following ISO 14040 standards (ISO, 2006). All model input parameters were compiled using U.S. LCI database values (NREL, 2010a) unless otherwise noted. The model results are intended to provide both swine producers and environmental practitioners with a compre- hensive overview of environmental impacts associated with operations typical for this region.

Uncertainty associated with the impacts was determined by Monte Carlo analysis assuming normal distribution with a variance not exceeding 1.5 for up to 1,000 model runs, as recommended by the SimaPro users manual, and presented as 95% confidence interval associated with mean impact value. A random value was chosen for each network flow, with the resulting range of all calculations providing a specific degree of uncertainty.

11: The environmental impacts assessed and presented for this study were climate change, terrestrial acidification, freshwa- ter and marine eutrophication, and terrestrial ecotoxicity. Impacts assessment characterizations were determined using ReCiPe 2008 (Geodkoop et al., 2009), an EU-based assessment methodology that combines the strengths of previous mid- point (e.g., CML 2000) and endpoint (Eco-indicator 99)

assessment approaches. Climate change was categorized as a midpoint (in kg CO2 equivalents) human health impact, and terrestrial acidification, freshwater and marine eutrophica- tion, and terrestrial ecotoxicity were assumed to be the most important midpoint ecosystem quality impacts for this study. A system-wide damage assessment on human health, eco- system diversity, and resource availability was also per- formed using ReCiPe 2008, representing the environmental relevance of each impact category assessed. Hierarchist per- spective contribution of human health and ecosystem diver- sity was assumed, based upon the most common policy principles with respect to assessment time-frame (Geodkoop et al., 2009).
12: An LCA model was developed to determine the environ- mental impact results for each of the five feed manufacturing and swine production facility processes discussed within the inventory analysis and shown within the system boundaries in Fig 1. A summary of the impact results determined is provided in Table 5, and further discussion for each impact category is provided below.
13: Climate change impacts were determined for each of the five feed manufacturing and production facility processes (Fig. 2). Swine enteric emissions resulted in the greatest con- tribution toward climate change (332 kg CO2 eq per head of swine) of the five processes analyzed, with manure emissions and handling being the second highest contributor (53.4kg CO2 eq per head of swine). These results highlight the im- portance of emissions minimization when considering bene- ficial changes associated with pork production.

Our results, in general, are in accordance with on-farm emissions and climate change data reported elsewhere, although the exclusion of feedstuff production complicates direct comparisons with other studies. For example, Edwards- Jones et al. (2009) reported that methane enteric emissions from sheep and cattle (708.8 to 2308.1 CO2 eq/ha/year) were significantly higher than manure emissions (25.9 to 79.5 CO2 eq/ha/year). Peters et al. (2010) also reported that the primary source of GHG emissions was enteric methanogenesis for their study of Australian red meat production (0.18 CH4/ head of cattle/day) and that 38% reduction of enteric methane emissions occurred when utilizing grain instead of grass feed. For Danish pig production, Dalgaard et al. (2007) reported a surprisingly low contribution of enteric methane production

(0.5kg CH4/100 kg live weight pig) compared with manure emissions (1.9kg CH4/100 kg live weight pig), which con- tradict findings from this study and others. It should be noted that GHG swine enteric emissions reported within this study do not account for CO2 adsorbed or otherwise removed from the atmosphere by plant growth during the production of feed. As a result, our climate change emissions are likely overreported because of our omission of feed production within the system boundaries; our methane emissions are similar to other studies. GHG emissions from manure han- dling systems are highly dependent upon manure solids content, temperature, surface area-to-volume ratio, and age and may be especially sensitive to ammonia emissions (Dal- gaard et al., 2007). These findings reflect the unique operating and climatic conditions associated with the NGP region compared with the southeastern United States, where the use of outdoor lagoons (with higher ambient temperatures) would be expected to have higher GHG emissions. Manure lagoons were also found to be a significant source of GHG emissions in Australia (Wiedemann et al., 2010). When in- cluding both on- and off-site production-related processes, feed production was reported as the most significant con- tributor toward climate change within the entire pork supply chain [2.0kg CO2 eq/kg pork (Dalgaard et al., 2007)], whereas on-site climate change emissions from facility operations (0.15kg CO2 eq/kg pork) were primarily attributed to elec- tricity and heating use.

As a result, our climate change emissions are likely overreported because of our omission of feed production within the system boundaries; our methane emissions are similar to other studies. GHG emissions from manure han- dling systems are highly dependent upon manure solids content, temperature, surface area-to-volume ratio, and age and may be especially sensitive to ammonia emissions (Dal- gaard et al., 2007). These findings reflect the unique operating and climatic conditions associated with the NGP region compared with the southeastern United States, where the use of outdoor lagoons (with higher ambient temperatures) would be expected to have higher GHG emissions. Manure lagoons were also found to be a significant source of GHG emissions in Australia (Wiedemann et al., 2010). When in- cluding both on- and off-site production-related processes, feed production was reported as the most significant con- tributor toward climate change within the entire pork supply chain [2.0kg CO2 eq/kg pork (Dalgaard et al., 2007)], whereas on-site climate change emissions from facility operations (0.15kg CO2 eq/kg pork) were primarily attributed to elec- tricity and heating use.
16: Eutrophication impacts were calculated in terms of [kg P eq] (freshwater) and [kg N eq] (marine), and the impacts of macronutrients nitrogen and phosphorous were integrated following the recommendations by Guinee et al. (2002) and Lindors et al. (1995). For freshwater eutrophication, produc- tion facility electricity and heating requirements were the largest contributors (0.00016kg P eq per head of swine), whereas manure emissions and handling was the largest contributor toward marine eutrophication (0.049kg N eq per head of swine), followed by enteric emissions (0.012kg N eq per head of swine), facility electricity and heating (0.010kg N eq per head of swine), and feed manufacturing (0.010kg N eq per head of swine). Comparison between these results and other studies can be difficult because of differing system boundaries or functional units. Dalgaard et al. (2007) reported nitrate as the primary contributor toward eutrophication (47 NO3 eq/kg Danish pork) for pig housing operations and that grain production and cultivation had the greatest contribu- tion toward eutrophication. Similarly, Eriksson et al. (2005)
