2: <header>Terrestrial acidification </header>

Acidification impacts were calculated in terms of [kg SO2 eq], and the predominant contributor of these impacts was manure emissions and handling (1.28kg SO2 eq per head of swine), followed by enteric emissions (0.32 kg SO2 eq per head of swine). These findings are similar to other studies that report production stages (i.e., building operations and manure emissions) to be the dominant contributors toward acidification. For example, pig housing was the largest con- tributor toward acidification (~24g SO2 eq/kg pork) and was attributed to ammonia emissions (Dalgaard et al., 2007), whereas feed production was also a significant acidifica- tion contributor. Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) re- ported between 0.0226 and 0.0435 kg SO2 eq/kg pork, with significant but uncertain contributions (64%-71%) resulting from operations and manure handling. A large degree of uncertainty was also found for our acidification results, primarily because of manure emissions and handling pro- cesses. Ammonia emissions from animals and manure pro- cesses accounted for 99% of acidification impacts (Eriksson et al., 2005) and was influenced by reducing nitrogen ex- cretion and implementing best practice manure manage- ment strategies for manure storage and spreading (e.g., injection).
3: <header>Terrestrial ecotoxicity </header>

Ecotoxicity was calculated in terms of kg 1,4-dichloroben- zene (kg 1,4-DB) used as a reference substance in the mid- point calculations (Geodkoop et al., 2009) and is primarily attributed toward heavy metal releases such as zinc, lead, and nickel emissions. Production facility electricity and heating was found to have the greatest ecotoxicity impacts (0.00060 kg 1,4-DB eq per head of swine) of the five processes evaluated and was primarily attributed to the energy port- folio common to South Dakota (Table 1). Ecotoxicity has been rarely reported within the swine production LCA lit- erature because of methodological limitations (Dalgaard et al., 2007). Ecotoxicity impacts did not account for the presence or release of "emerging" contaminants that would be expected to be found within swine manure, such as an- tibiotics, antimicrobials (Dewey et al., 1999; USDA, 2007), and hormones (Raman et ,2004). The reader is encouraged to review the paper by Mackie et al. (2006) for an overview of these emerging contaminants within agricultural systems, as their potential impacts to the immediate soil environment could have significant environmental consequences.
4: The three damage assessment categories shown in Fig. 3 provide characterized cumulative environmental effects from production operations for human health, ecosystem diversity, and resource availability. For human health, swine enteric emissions resulted in 75.9% of the overall impacts, with manure emissions and handling responsible for an additional 19.0% of the cumulative impacts. Ecosys- tem diversity was also significantly impacted by enteric emissions (82.3%), whereas resources impacts were influ- enced by production facility electricity and propane use (81.5%). The remaining processes resulted in minor contri- butions.

FIG. 3. Characterized damage assessment summaries of human health, ecosystem diversity, and resources availability impacts associated with feed manufacturing and swine pro- duction facility operations for normal operations, antimicro- bial use, reduction of completed feed transport, and increased renewable energy use. Data presented represent average analysis results in the absence of the uncertainty analysis. Raw data is provided in Supplementary Data Table S2.
5: <header>Scenario Analysis </header>

A scenario analysis was performed to determine whether or how specific operational changes would influence life cycle impacts determined for the baseline, normal operations sce- nario. Changes to normal operations considered included:

1. Antimicrobial feed additive use: Chlortetracycline (CTC) is a commonly administered feed additive anti- microbial (Dewey et al., 1999; USDA, 2007) typically fed at all stages of swine growth, but most frequently ad- ministered in feeds of immature swine to enhance feed utilization and growth promotion. The use of CTC feed additive has been previously reported to reduce ma- nure carbon dioxide and methane emissions by 27.8% through the inhibition of specific aceticlastic and me- thanogenic bacteria commonly found in swine manure storage lagoons and pits (Stone et al., 2009) and thus would be expected to reduce climate change impacts. This scenario assumed that CTC was administered at a subtherapeutic dose (22 mg CTC/kg body weight) and that manure carbon dioxide and methane emis- sions were reduced by 28% within the manure emissions and handling processes prior to land appli- cation. Antimicrobial manufacturing, transport to the feed manufacturing facility, feed blending operations, and environmental impacts associated with land ap- plication of CTC-containing manure were not consid- ered within this scenario, although each of these processes would be considered contributors toward human health, ecosystem quality, and resources im- pacts based upon similar manufacturing data (Nielsen et al., 2007) for enzyme production and environmental fate and transport within soil systems (Clay et al., 2005; Kumar et al., 2005; Mackie et al., 2006). The benefits of

Impacts include terrestrial ecotoxicity (kg 1,4-DB eq), freshwater (kg P eq) and marine (kg N eq) eutrophication, terrestrial acidification (kg SO2 eq), and climate change im- pacts (kg CO2 eq) for feed manufacturing and swine pro- duction facility operations (manure storage and handling, enteric emissions, electricity generation and propane pro- duction, and water extraction). CTC use discussed above were reported to not offset the negative global impacts associated with CTC manufacturing and transport (Stone et al., 2010);

2. Reduction in completed feed transport distance: The transport distance for the completed feed was reduced by 50% (32.2km) to determine the benefit of using a local compared with regional feed manufacturing fa-

<list>cility. The availability of local feedstuffs for use in swine feeds in the NGP reduces the financial and environ- mental burdens associated with feedstuff transport. Many NGP production operations use local feedstuff supplies when available to reduce financial and envi- ronmental burdens associated with feedstuff transport; 
3. Increase in renewable energy use: A 30% renewable energy portfolio (addition of 15% wind and 15% solar replacing coal energy) to both the feed manufacturing and swine production facility energy portfolio was as- sumed. Many production facilities in the NGP region are situated at remote locations far from traditional energy production or use markets, and this region also has a high potential for both wind and solar energy production (NREL, 2010b). Current regional and na- tional tax incentives are providing financial incentives for facilities to implement small-scale and local renew- able energy production (US-DOE, 2010), and 30% re- newable use was assumed to be a long-term goal for this region.</list>

Results from these three scenarios compared with the normal operations baseline model results are shown in Figs. 3 and 4. For climate change impacts, all three scenarios resulted in a slight reduction of CO2 eq emissions (3.7%, 1.4%, and 0.3% for antimicrobial use, reduced feed transport, and re- newable energy use, respectively), with the greatest benefit occurring for manure emissions handling associated with the antimicrobial use scenario because of reduced carbon dioxide and methane emissions (Stone et al., 2009). Antimicrobial use also resulted in 21.4% reduction of terrestrial acidification impacts, and 16.7% reduction in marine eutrophication im- pacts. For reduced feed transport, the benefits were relatively minor (greatest benefit was 5.9% reduction in marine eutro- phication) and associated with feed manufacturing. The mi- nor benefits associated with reduced feed transport agree with the results by Dalgaard et al. (2007), who report "food miles" to be a misleading environmental indicator, as Danish transport was reported as an insignificant (<1%) component of total GHG emissions for pork production. The greatest impacts enhancements occurred for the increased renewable energy scenario, where terrestrial ecotoxicity (66.4%) and freshwater eutrophication (89.3%) benefits occurred for facil- ity electricity and heating requirements. These enhancements further highlight the benefits associated with on-site renew- able energy implementation.

Antimicrobial feed additive use: Chlortetracycline (CTC) is a commonly administered feed additive anti- microbial (Dewey et al., 1999; USDA, 2007) typically fed at all stages of swine growth, but most frequently ad- ministered in feeds of immature swine to enhance feed utilization and growth promotion. The use of CTC feed additive has been previously reported to reduce ma- nure carbon dioxide and methane emissions by 27.8% through the inhibition of specific aceticlastic and me- thanogenic bacteria commonly found in swine manure storage lagoons and pits (Stone et al., 2009) and thus would be expected to reduce climate change impacts. This scenario assumed that CTC was administered at a subtherapeutic dose (22 mg CTC/kg body weight) and that manure carbon dioxide and methane emis- sions were reduced by 28% within the manure emissions and handling processes prior to land appli- cation. Antimicrobial manufacturing, transport to the feed manufacturing facility, feed blending operations, and environmental impacts associated with land ap- plication of CTC-containing manure were not consid- ered within this scenario, although each of these processes would be considered contributors toward human health, ecosystem quality, and resources im- pacts based upon similar manufacturing data (Nielsen et al., 2007) for enzyme production and environmental fate and transport within soil systems (Clay et al., 2005; Kumar et al., 2005; Mackie et al., 2006). The benefits of

FIG. 4. Summary of scenario analysis results impacts re- sults for normal operations, antimicrobial use, reduction of completed feed transport, and increased renewable energy use. Impacts include terrestrial ecotoxicity (kg 1,4-DB eq), freshwater (kg P eq) and marine (kg N eq) eutrophication, terrestrial acidification (kg SO2 eq), and climate change im- pacts (kg CO2 eq) for feed manufacturing and swine pro- duction facility operations (manure storage and handling, enteric emissions, electricity generation and propane pro- duction, and water extraction). Error bars represent 95% confidence interval of respective impact from Monte Carlo analysis assuming normal distribution.
CTC use discussed above were reported to not offset the negative global impacts associated with CTC manufacturing and transport (Stone et al., 2010);

2. Reduction in completed feed transport distance: The transport distance for the completed feed was reduced by 50% (32.2km) to determine the benefit of using a local compared with regional feed manufacturing fa-

<list>cility. The availability of local feedstuffs for use in swine feeds in the NGP reduces the financial and environ- mental burdens associated with feedstuff transport. Many NGP production operations use local feedstuff supplies when available to reduce financial and envi- ronmental burdens associated with feedstuff transport; 
3. Increase in renewable energy use: A 30% renewable energy portfolio (addition of 15% wind and 15% solar replacing coal energy) to both the feed manufacturing and swine production facility energy portfolio was as- sumed. Many production facilities in the NGP region are situated at remote locations far from traditional energy production or use markets, and this region also has a high potential for both wind and solar energy production (NREL, 2010b). Current regional and na- tional tax incentives are providing financial incentives for facilities to implement small-scale and local renew- able energy production (US-DOE, 2010), and 30% re- newable use was assumed to be a long-term goal for this region.</list>

Results from these three scenarios compared with the normal operations baseline model results are shown in Figs. 3 and 4. For climate change impacts, all three scenarios resulted in a slight reduction of CO2 eq emissions (3.7%, 1.4%, and 0.3% for antimicrobial use, reduced feed transport, and re- newable energy use, respectively), with the greatest benefit occurring for manure emissions handling associated with the antimicrobial use scenario because of reduced carbon dioxide and methane emissions (Stone et al., 2009). Antimicrobial use also resulted in 21.4% reduction of terrestrial acidification impacts, and 16.7% reduction in marine eutrophication im- pacts. For reduced feed transport, the benefits were relatively minor (greatest benefit was 5.9% reduction in marine eutro- phication) and associated with feed manufacturing. The mi- nor benefits associated with reduced feed transport agree with the results by Dalgaard et al. (2007), who report "food miles" to be a misleading environmental indicator, as Danish transport was reported as an insignificant (<1%) component of total GHG emissions for pork production. The greatest impacts enhancements occurred for the increased renewable energy scenario, where terrestrial ecotoxicity (66.4%) and freshwater eutrophication (89.3%) benefits occurred for facil- ity electricity and heating requirements. These enhancements further highlight the benefits associated with on-site renew- able energy implementation.
10: <header>Scenario Analysis </header>

11: In this study, an LCA was performed for a hypothetical modern NGP U.S. swine production facility to determine the environmental impacts associated with typical facility oper- ations. Although the exclusion of feedstuff production within our study's system boundaries complicates direct compari- sons with other studies, swine enteric emissions and manure storage and handling were consistently the largest climate change contributors, thus highlighting the importance for facility operations to consider alternative emissions capture, treatment, or handling processes to reduce their associated environmental burdens. Climate change impacts were slightly reduced when including antimicrobial CTC subther- apeutic feed additive use, although system boundaries did

not include CTC manufacturing and transport impacts, which would be expected to contribute toward human health, eco- system diversity, and resource availability impacts. Electricity generation and propane production impacts were major contributors toward ecotoxicity and eutrophication impacts, although these impacts were significantly reduced when considering local renewable energy within the facility power supply portfolio. Results from our scenario analyses suggest that the operations modifications considered within this study provide marginal environmental enhancements, except those associated with antimicrobial use, and further advo- cates the use of local renewable energy for providing the greatest environmental benefit for swine production. Al- though the focus of this study was solely on NGP swine production facility operations (i.e., gate-to-gate perspective) using a common South Dakota energy portfolio, and excluded likely significant impacts associated facility feedstuff pro- duction (Eriksson et al., 2005), the life cycle impacts deter- mined provide NGP swine producers and environmental practitioners with an operations critique that can be used to improve swine production environmental efficiencies and identify opportunities for a future carbon-constrained and sustainability-aware marketplace.

Although the exclusion of feedstuff production within our study's system boundaries complicates direct compari- sons with other studies, swine enteric emissions and manure storage and handling were consistently the largest climate change contributors, thus highlighting the importance for facility operations to consider alternative emissions capture, treatment, or handling processes to reduce their associated environmental burdens. Climate change impacts were slightly reduced when including antimicrobial CTC subther- apeutic feed additive use, although system boundaries did

not include CTC manufacturing and transport impacts, which would be expected to contribute toward human health, eco- system diversity, and resource availability impacts. Electricity generation and propane production impacts were major contributors toward ecotoxicity and eutrophication impacts, although these impacts were significantly reduced when considering local renewable energy within the facility power supply portfolio. Results from our scenario analyses suggest that the operations modifications considered within this study provide marginal environmental enhancements, except those associated with antimicrobial use, and further advo- cates the use of local renewable energy for providing the greatest environmental benefit for swine production. Al- though the focus of this study was solely on NGP swine production facility operations (i.e., gate-to-gate perspective) using a common South Dakota energy portfolio, and excluded likely significant impacts associated facility feedstuff pro- duction (Eriksson et al., 2005), the life cycle impacts deter- mined provide NGP swine producers and environmental practitioners with an operations critique that can be used to improve swine production environmental efficiencies and identify opportunities for a future carbon-constrained and sustainability-aware marketplace.
